does: This paper identifies and formalizes semantic attacks targeting tool descriptors in the Model Context Protocol (MCP), which enables LLMs to invoke external tools. The authors introduce three attack classes—Tool Poisoning (malicious tool descriptions), Shadowing (impersonating legitimate tools), and Rug Pull (deceptive capability claims)—and demonstrate how descriptor-level manipulation can bias tool selection and reasoning despite intact prompt defenses. They propose a layered defense architecture combining descriptor integrity verification, semantic vetting via auxiliary LLM analysis, and runtime guardrails, all deployable without model retraining. Evaluation on GPT-4, DeepSeek-V3, and LLaMA-3.5 across eight prompting strategies in adversarial MCP scenarios quantifies attack success rates and defense effectiveness.
connects: This work extends the growing body of research on LLM safety and adversarial robustness by shifting focus from prompt injection attacks—the dominant concern in tool-use security—to a previously underexplored descriptor-level threat model. It builds on prior work examining how LLMs interpret and act on tool metadata, connecting to research on context pollution, model hallucination in reasoning contexts, and supply-chain attacks in AI pipelines. The paper responds to the reality that MCP deployments treat tool descriptions as trusted despite their direct influence on model behavior, paralleling earlier discoveries of how LLMs can be misled through benign-looking input contamination. The defense approach combines classical integrity mechanisms with LLM-based semantic analysis, drawing from established multi-layer security paradigms.
enables: This work establishes descriptor-level threat modeling as a critical frontier for tool-augmented LLM security, opening investigation into other metadata-driven attack surfaces in multi-agent and autonomous systems. It enables developers to implement practical defenses for existing MCP deployments without retraining, lowering barriers to hardening real-world systems. Future work can extend the threat model to other protocol-based tool integrations, explore adaptive attacks against the proposed defenses, and investigate whether descriptor attacks generalize across different LLM architectures and reasoning styles. The formalization of attack classes provides a foundation for standardizing descriptor security requirements in emerging protocols, potentially influencing how tool metadata is specified and validated in future agent frameworks.
